summary: My great-grandparents Vittoria and Michelangelo Venditto both came from Pietraroja, a town in Benevento, Italy. They migrated separately to New York City in the early twentieth century, seeking better opportunities. Pietraroja was the place they left behind, and it connects me to my Italian roots as I researched my family's migration history.

Benevento is the province in Italy where my great-grandparents Vittoria and Michelangelo Venditto originated, specifically from Pietraroja. They left Benevento in the early 1900s to start new lives in New York City. This place is central to my family’s story of immigration and the transatlantic ties that have shaped my identity.

Italy is the country my ancestors came from, including my great-grandparents who left southern Italy in the early twentieth century. I’ve traveled there many times for my research on Italian immigration history and to reconnect with family. Italy today is very different from the Italy they left; it has become a destination for immigrants, facing many of the same challenges my family once did.

New York City is where my great-grandparents settled after leaving Italy in the early 1900s. It’s where they married, raised children, and became American citizens while maintaining strong connections to Italy. I grew up in New York’s large Italian American community, which deeply influenced my sense of identity and inspired my academic journey.

Cassano delle Murge is the town in Apulia, Italy, where my great-grandmother Margherita Giordano was from before she migrated to New York City. Her migration story runs parallel to my other great-grandparents’ journeys from southern Italy. This place represents part of my paternal grandmother’s heritage and the broader Apulian roots in my family.

Apulia is the region in southeastern Italy where my great-grandmother Margherita Giordano and great-grandfather Eustachio Cassano were born, in Cassano delle Murge and Acquaviva delle Fonti respectively. They both migrated to New York City around the same time as my other ancestors. Apulia is a key part of my family’s Italian heritage and migration story.

Acquaviva delle Fonti is the neighboring town in Apulia, Italy, where my great-grandfather Eustachio Cassano was from before migrating to New York City. His journey alongside my great-grandmother Margherita Giordano’s migration from nearby Cassano delle Murge connects me to this region. This place is part of the rich Italian background I explored during my studies and travels.

I attended the University of Notre Dame from 2004 to 2008 for my undergraduate studies. It was the first step in my academic journey away from New York City, where I grew up. Notre Dame helped me begin exploring my identity and interests, which later led me to pursue a Ph.D. in migration history.

I earned my Ph.D. in migration history at the University of Minnesota between 2008 and 2014. This period was crucial for deepening my research on Italian immigration and reconnecting with my family’s past. The university provided the resources and environment for me to explore the complex stories of migration that shaped my family and many others.
